title: The tags that say what they are
testCase: Are there <header>, <nav>, <main> and <footer> instead of the boxes?
blocks.0.text: Here is the soup again. This time we clean it up.
blocks.1.text: You will not learn anything hard. You will learn seven words. That is all.
blocks.3.items.0: <header> — the top. Logo, title, menu. Whatever sits at the top of every page.
blocks.3.items.1: <nav> — the navigation. You already know this one.
blocks.3.items.2: <main> — the MAIN content. The thing the visitor came for. Only one per page.
blocks.3.items.3: <article> — a standalone thing that makes sense on its own. A listing, an article, a comment. If you could cut it out and paste it elsewhere — it is an article.
blocks.3.items.4: <section> — a section of something. A part of a whole, usually with its own heading.
blocks.3.items.5: <aside> — to the side of the main thing. Related but not essential: a sidebar, a "see also", an ad.
blocks.4.text: Every one of them behaves EXACTLY like <div>. They are blocks, they take a whole line, they look like nothing. There is one difference: they mean something.
blocks.7.text: Read it. Now read the other one above. The result on screen is identical — but one of them you understand in five seconds, and the other you have to decipher.
blocks.8.text: Fourteen boxes became four tags that explain themselves.
blocks.9.text: Did you notice the other thing
blocks.10.text:
Tags you have known for a long time came back.
became

.
became

.
became

.

became .
blocks.11.text: The soup was not made of <div> alone. It was made of refusing to think about what a thing IS. The heading was a heading all along — somebody was just in a hurry.
blocks.13.text: Above I drew in the frames and the names so you can see the skeleton. Your page will not show them — semantic tags are invisible, exactly like <div>.
blocks.14.text: And two more, small but useful
blocks.15.text: You write "12 July". A human understands. A machine sees two words. And it wants to sort by date, show "3 days ago", put the event in a calendar.
blocks.17.text: The human reads what you wrote. The machine reads the datetime — in one strict format it understands. The same pairing again: one for the human, one for the machine.
blocks.18.text: And <address> — for contact details. Not for every address on the page, but for yours: how to reach you or the author of the article. It usually lives inside the <footer>.
blocks.20.text: Did you notice the logo stayed <div class="logo">? On purpose. There is no "logo" tag. When NO suitable tag exists — that is when <div> is the right choice, and that is exactly why it exists.
blocks.21.text: <div> is not forbidden. It is the last resort. Ask yourself: "is there a tag that says what this is?" If yes — use it. If not — <div>, with a clear conscience.
blocks.23.text: Everyone hesitates here, including people who have done this for years. Ask: "if I cut this out and put it on another site, would it make sense on its own?" The black bike listing — yes, it would. So: article. A section called "How to order" — no, without the page around it, it is meaningless. So: section.
blocks.24.text: And if you get it wrong, the world will not end. A misplaced <article> is better than yet another <div>.
blocks.25.text: Your turn. Clean up the page. Every box that has a real tag — swap it. Leave the rest.
